data.title: Bruker: Attractive Valuation Ahead Of An Earnings Inflection
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Bruker (BRKR) is positioned for good growth as the company enters FY26. The company should benefit from a healthy backlog, easier Y/Y comparisons, and recovery in key markets, including academic and government, biopharma, and China. In addition, around $40 mn of high-margin semiconductor orders that shifted from FY25 to FY26 should also help revenue growth. On the margin front, the company should benefit from operating leverage on higher revenues, cost reduction actions, and improving pricing realisation and mix. Further, easing of M&A related dilution should help margins. Despite improving fundamentals, Bruker continues to trade at significant discounts versus both its historical valuation and peers in the life science tools space. With double-digit EPS growth expected from FY26 onward, the stock offers an attractive risk-reward profile driven by earnings growth alone. There is potential for re-rating as the company continues with its good execution and the market recovers.

Recent Financial Performance

Bruker's Q3 FY25 revenues declined 0.5% year-over-year. Excluding the impact of acquisitions and currency effects, the organic decline was 4.5% year-over-year. The drop mainly came from soft demand in the academic and government market, which significantly impacted the scientific instruments business. Partially offsetting this impact was a 1.1% benefit from acquisitions and a 2.9% benefit from FX.

Adjusted gross margin declined 110 basis points to 50.1%. Adjusted operating margin contracted 260 basis points year-over-year to 12.3%. The decline was a result of operating deleverage, tariff costs, and an unfavorable mix.

Outlook

I am optimistic about Bruker’s revenue and margin growth outlook moving forward. In FY25, the company faced headwinds from its largest end-market, academic and government, due to an uncertain funding environment in the US. In addition, Chinese demand remained low, and biopharma companies also postponed capex due to uncertainty. The situation in FY26 looks much better, with the company benefiting from a healthy backlog, easier comparisons, and healthy demand across its end-markets. In addition, the company's cost-cutting initiatives and improved mix should also help margins.

Bruker enters FY26 with good revenue visibility. Management reported around seven months of backlog in Q3. Since then, the end-market conditions have improved, especially in biopharma, as uncertainty around MFN pricing negotiations has largely cleared. I believe this improved sentiment should further help the company improve its backlog when it reports its fourth-quarter results and enters FY26. Also, book-to-bill was greater than 1 in Q3, which indicates orders are running ahead of sales and should help build backlog.

In addition, around $40 million of semiconductor orders that were supposed to be shipped in late FY25 slipped into FY26 due to customer timing issues. As these orders get converted into revenues in FY26, they should help sales growth. The semiconductor metrology is one of Bruker’s higher-margin businesses. As those orders are delivered, both revenues and margins should benefit.

Many of the company’s end markets that impacted FY25 results have recently shown signs of a recovery. The academic and government markets, which comprise Bruker’s biggest market with about 40% of sales, were affected last year with concerns over U.S. federal government funding. It is stabilizing now, and Bruker has seen about $10 million of NIH and NSF-funded orders in Q3, which includes advanced NMR systems sold to Northwestern University and the University of Delaware. With federal funding back in the pipeline and easier comparisons from lower sales in FY25, the US academic and government markets should shift from a headwind to a tailwind in FY26. Outside the U.S., trends in this market are also picking up. Europe showed healthy demand after a weak period, while Japan and Korea have stepped up their buying of material science and academic instruments. So I am expecting a good growth in this market in FY26.

Biopharma, which is around mid-teens percentage of revenue, is also turning after seeing uncertainty around MFN pricing and broader capex caution. Bruker posted double-digit organic order growth from biopharma clients in the third quarter, and I expect the growth to improve as this clarity emerges further. The company should also benefit from its new product launches, particularly in life science mass spectrometry, where recently launched platforms introduced in mid-2025 are already seeing early traction and helping demand beyond simple replacement cycles.

China, which is around 14% of revenues, is also showing signs of improvement after delayed stimulus funding impacted the last couple of years. Management noted that $6 mn of orders in the third quarter were directly related to the government stimulus money. As China continues to invest in domestic drug discovery, applied research, and industrial markets, it should return to growth in FY 2026 and beyond.

On the margin front, the company should benefit from around $100-$120 mn in cost reduction, which is roughly 10% of operating expenses. Around 95% of these cost actions are already done or underway, and we should see good benefits from the current year onwards. The company is targeting both operating expenses and cost of goods sold under this cost reduction initiative that includes supply chain/procurement optimization, improving manufacturing efficiency, headcount rationalization, administrative simplification, and lean actions. Around 60% of the savings is expected to come from operating expenses, while 40% is expected to come from the cost of goods sold.

The execution risk around these cost reductions is also low, as Bruker has already secured worker council approvals in Germany and France (where labour rules are strict) and begun executing the majority of these initiatives.

In addition to cost reduction actions, margins should also benefit from improved pricing and a better sales mix. In addition to price increases in response to tariffs, Bruker is also doing structural price increases, especially in areas where it has a strong competitive positioning and technology differentiation, like mass spectrometry and ultra-high field NMR systems. In addition, as discussed earlier, a favorable mix shift toward higher-margin semiconductor and biopharma revenue in FY26 should further support margin expansion.

One thing that adversely impacted margins in FY25 was M&As. Acquisitions reduced margins by about 60 basis points in FY25. However, on its last earnings call, management noted that the ELITech acquisition is now beating the acquisition model expectations, and M&A drag that the company had during FY25 should be near break-even in FY26.

Overall healthy backlog, recovering end markets, shipment of delayed high-margin semiconductor products, operating leverage, cost reduction, disciplined pricing, and better product mix should help Bruker post good revenue and margin growth in FY26.

Valuation

Bruker is trading at 24.47x FY26 consensus EPS estimate of $2.15 and 21.26x FY27 consensus EPS estimate of $2.45. This is below its historical 5-year average forward P/E of 28.06x. The stock is also trading at a discount vs. other life science tool peers like Danaher (DHR), trading at 28.09x FY26 consensus EPS estimate, and Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), trading at 25.17x FY26 consensus EPS estimate.

I believe this discount is mainly due to the headwinds that the company faced in FY25 related to disruption in US academic and government funding, softer biopharma capex, and margin pressure due to slower growth and M&As. However, with the outlook improving in FY26, I believe some of this discount should narrow. Academic and government funding conditions are expected to improve. Biopharma has turned positive with double-digit order growth in Q3. China is also showing signs of a turnaround. In addition, roughly $40 million of semiconductor orders shifted from FY25 to FY26, which should benefit both revenue and margin growth in the current year. So, I see a good chance of the company's P/E multiple re-rating.

However, even if the P/E multiple stays at the current levels, the strong double-digit growth over the next few years should result in a good upside for shareholders. The company's EPS is expected to deliver double-digit growth over the next several years, driven by revenue growth, cost reduction actions, and price/mix benefit. So, overall risk/reward looks favorable at the current level, and the stock can see good upside. Hence, I have a buy rating.

Risks

My thesis anticipates the academic and government end markets, particularly in the US, to see recovery after seeing disruptions in FY25. However, renewed delays in grant administration, government shutdowns, or unfavorable regulatory and policy developments could negatively impact orders and revenue growth.

There is execution risk related to its cost reduction plan. While the company is doing a good job in this area, any operational issues or lower-than-expected savings from these plans could negatively impact margin growth.

Takeaway

While FY25 was a tough year for the company, Bruker is entering FY26 with an improved outlook. The company's revenue should benefit from a strong backlog, recovering end market, and $40 mn of high-margin semiconductor orders that shifted from FY25 to FY26. Margins should benefit from operating leverage, cost reduction, and price/mix improvements. Valuation looks attractive as well at a discount versus historical levels and peers. Hence, I rate BRKR stock a buy.

data.title: ITA Vs. XAR: How To Play Donald Trump's Military Shake-Up
data.description:
The last few days have likely felt like whiplash for investors in the defense industry. The year started off with the US military invading Venezuela. In an article that was just published, I talked about the ramifications of that from the perspective of the oil industry. In that article, I discussed the possibility of an escalation in that conflict, though I do think the probability of that occurring is quite low at this point.

Such an escalation would prove bullish for the defense industry. But then, almost immediately after that, news began circulating that Donald Trump is considering an illegal seizure of Greenland, even if that means doing so militarily. This would be a violation of NATO and technically could trigger a major military conflict. In all likelihood, however, a move of that nature would also be without any major step up in activity for the defense industry. And that's because other NATO members would be unlikely to militarily retaliate against the US government. Sanctions would be the more likely path to take.

While these developments will probably have more of a neutral impact on the defense industry, any sort of military escalation would prove bullish for the sector. But that's not where things ended. On January 7th, the White House reported that Donald Trump signed an executive order aimed at stopping stock buybacks and ‘excessive corporate distributions’ amongst military contractors.

Although the executive order does not do this outright, it does push the Secretary of Defense to identify defense contractors that underperform, that fail to invest their own capital in production capacity, and that might otherwise fall short of what's needed in order for the US military to behave optimally. This on its own is a net negative for the sector. But just as this development came out, a positive push came from the White House, with Donald Trump calling for a surge in the military budget for the 2027 year. The end result here was a jump in share prices for some defense contractors. But at the end of the day, the ultimate impact will depend on the details.

This is a slew of activities that needs to be focused on. In the worst case, one where any sort of proposed surge in military spending does not materialize but restrictions on defense contractors are implemented, many companies in the sector could see short-term pain. But because these developments would cause what I believe to be a more efficient use of capital, the longer-term picture would be a net positive. And in the event that we see a surge in military spending, regardless of whether or not recent geopolitical issues inflame and cause actual conflict, the impact could be decisively positive for the sector.

How to play the defense sector today

As tempting as it is to play fortune teller, I believe that speculating on military conflict increasing in the near term is not a terribly productive use of time or energy. Nobody knows what the future holds, and while I do think risks of some major international conflict are greater now than they have been in years, there really isn't much that can be said there that I haven't mentioned already in this article without engaging in rampant speculation. So instead of focusing on that, I will instead direct my efforts to what we do know.

First and foremost, it is a known fact that defense contractors have a long history of going over budget and taking far longer than expected on major projects. The most notorious example of this is the F-35 Lightning II Joint Strike Fighter Program. This fighter jet program, which is being headed by Lockheed Martin (LMT), began in the 1990s, and it was estimated that development and procurement would cost somewhere between $200 billion and $300 billion. The current lifetime cost today is in excess of $1.7 trillion, with some estimates pegging it at $2 trillion in the future.

Now, to be fair, this is not a complete overage in and of itself. There definitely were cost overruns. For instance, the original program had a significantly reduced scope compared to what the program has today. Plus, there were certain foibles that could have been avoided. The Pentagon began mass production before testing was completed, which on its own created issues that added countless billions of dollars to the mix. There were also issues regarding sustainment, which is essentially the cost of owning and operating the aircraft. Those costs ballooned, especially because maintenance hours were significantly underestimated and because other costs and inefficiencies developed. But at the same time, there was also a significant increase in scope that added materially to the expense structure of the program.

In reality, cost overages for defense projects are often nowhere near that much. But they do often run materially over what is anticipated. This leads to legitimate concerns about waste that Donald Trump is tapping into. And the idea here is that the companies that will be paid for catering to the US military should be doing a better job and not rewarding shareholders materially if they aren't. I would venture to say that most people, on both sides of the political aisle, would agree with this view. And when we look at the data over the last few years, it is clear that something needs to change.

In the table above, you can see annual revenue for the years 2022 through 2024 for the five largest defense contractors in the US. This is not total revenue for the companies. Rather, using third-party estimates, it is the amount of revenue that they brought in each of those years specifically because of their defense operations. Over this time, we are looking at defense-related revenue of $623.8 billion.

Now, in order to be as comprehensive as possible, the rest of this data refers to results for the 2022 through 2024 fiscal years, as well as for the first nine months of 2025. So, there are some comparability issues here. But in the table above, you can see total dividends, share buybacks, and capital expenditures for these same five businesses. You can also see total corporate revenue for these windows of time and operating cash flow in the table below.

What this data illustrates is that, from 2022 through 2024 and for the first nine months of 2025, these five firms paid out $46.71 billion in dividends. They also allocated $33.54 billion toward buybacks. The data here also shows what the picture looks like when we remove Boeing (BA) from the mix. After all, that company has, as I have detailed in other articles, faced significant profitability issues over the last few years. In fact, from 2022 through the third quarter of 2025, it paid dividends of only $244 million. And that was specifically on some recently issued preferred stock. It has not engaged in any buybacks during this time. But the other companies, however, have engaged in a lot.

When all combined, we are looking at total cash returns to shareholders of $80.25 billion. But over that same window of time, those companies invested just $29.57 billion into capital initiatives. Some of that necessarily had to have been toward maintaining existing operations. So, some number less than that must have, by definition, been allocated toward growth. Regardless, this still means that the firms have been using not only revenue that they are generating from non-government sources but also from government agencies in order to transfer value to shareholders. In a world where budgets are being largely adhered to and timelines are being honored, this is not unreasonable. But the assertion of Donald Trump and those who support his view is that using taxpayer money to further the benefit of investors at the detriment of the American public is wrong.

Normally, the US government would not have the power to force changes when it comes to things like dividends and buybacks of publicly traded companies. Technically, the government can't do that. But it does have significant discretion over contracts. And according to the executive order signed into existence, any contractors that are found to have underperformed, invested inadequate amounts of capital into their production capacity, or otherwise fallen short of expectations will have the chance to submit a remediation plan in order to better comply with contract terms. If their plans are found to be inadequate or if the dispute remains unresolved, the executive order authorizes the Secretary of Defense to pursue other remedies, which might include amending contracts or invoking the Defense Production Act. I am not a legal expert, so I don't know how this will play out. But given the nature of what is going on here, the end result could be a net negative for companies in the defense industry.

Just as this seemed like it would turn out to be a problem for the industry, Donald Trump made clear on January 7th that he wants to see the 2027 U.S. government budget increase military spending to $1.5 trillion. He cited ‘troubled and dangerous times’ when making this declaration. Regardless of your views of what is going on from a foreign affairs perspective, I think we can all agree that the invasion of Venezuela, heightened tensions regarding Greenland, movements by China around Taiwan, and the unresolved war between Russia and Ukraine all serve as examples that we are indeed in a precarious moment in US diplomatic and military history.

To put this in perspective, over the last couple of decades, the US military budget has exploded. It grew from only $304 billion at the turn of the century to $962 billion for 2025. Interestingly, however, as a percent of the total U.S. government budget, it has actually gotten smaller. Back in the year 2000, it was 16.7% of the US budget. It ended up peaking at 21.9% in 2007. This was at the height of the War on Terror. From that point on until the year 2020, it dropped, eventually falling to 10%. Since then, however, it has been rising, hitting 13.7% for 2025.

An increase to $1.5 trillion would mean a jump in the course of two years of 55.9%. It is unclear, however, how this capital will be spent if it is ultimately given the green light. After all, this is something that will have to go through Congress. And it is not even a guarantee that it will come to fruition. If it does, however, there could be some big winners from this. And some of the biggest could be the same companies receiving pushback over their dividends and share buybacks. Interestingly, on the day that this development occurred, shares of Lockheed Martin jumped 4.3%. Say, rather, a substantial move higher on no fundamental news coming out. Northrop Grumman (NOC), which is the third-largest defense contractor, saw its share price pop 2.4%. And General Dynamics (GD) experienced an increase of 1.7%.

These are companies that I have written about in the past. Back in October of last year, for instance, I reaffirmed Lockheed Martin as a ‘hold’ candidate. I also called General Dynamics a ‘buy’ in September of last year. Boeing, which is one of the top five defense contractors out there, I reaffirmed as a ‘hold’ in November. But the other top five, Northrop Grumman and RTX Corporation (RTX), are companies that I have not written about in years, with the former last being written about in 2017 and the latter in 2019.

Technically, any of these firms could be interesting prospects on the idea that a surge in military spending might occur. But there are safer ways to play this if you believe it will come to fruition. A valid strategy would be to buy an ETF that tracks the defense industry. A prime example of this can be seen by looking at iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF (ITA). As the chart above illustrates, it is heavily weighted toward some of the major names in the defense sector. In total, there are 41 different holdings in the ETF. However, the top 10 comprise 75.3% of its overall weight. The weighted average market capitalization in the portfolio is $41.7 billion. And the median firm size is about $16 billion.

An alternative way to play this is by buying the State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR). The chart above shows its top 10 holdings. It also is quite diverse, with 40 holdings in total. But while the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF is top-heavy, the State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF is more evenly weighted. As the chart illustrates, it consists of more smaller companies. And the top ten largest holdings account for only 37.3% of total assets. Even so, the weighted average market capitalization in the ETF stands at a hefty $55.2 billion. But the median market value is $14.7 billion. This might be a better way to play for those who want less company-specific risk and the prospect of the benefits that come from investing in smaller companies. To be fair, both ETFs have performed exceptionally well over the last 12 months, with the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF up 56.3% while the State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF is up 57%.

Given that I am focused primarily right now on these two ETFs, I would say that some additional assessment of them is warranted. They do have some positive things going for them, but they also have some negative things. On the positive side, I do believe that the current Administration in the US is much more in favor of expanding the defense sector. The contents of this article demonstrate that much. 2026 is expected to be the highest spending year on record for the military. The requested amount was set at about $962 billion for the year. And even though that matches what was spent in 2025, the requested amount for 2025 was only $860 billion. So, we should expect continued growth here year over year. And with that, additional investments in defense-oriented companies should turn out to be a windfall.

This helps to explain why shares of these companies are trading so high. The price-to-earnings multiple of the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF is an astounding 45.9. The State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF is only a bit lower at 37.3. By comparison, the S&P 500 stands at 31.2, which is already very high. This also creates risk for investors. It is my belief that we are heading into a recession. This would not have an impact, at least in the near term, on defense spending. But the companies in these ETFs derive a lot of revenue from the commercial sector as well. This could cause some concern from investors, ultimately pushing the stock down. And honestly, it's because of these lofty valuations and these risks in light of broader economic conditions that have me taking a more neutral stance on them.

If you do decide to invest in them, there are a couple of things you should know. First and foremost, as the chart above illustrates, they have soundly outperformed the market over the last decade. While the S&P 500 is up 328.1% on a total return basis, the State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF is up 348.7%. Meanwhile, the iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF is up 480.2%. It is also worth noting that if you do decide to invest in these, the actual cost of doing so is modest. The iShares U.S. Aerospace & Defense ETF has an expense ratio of just 0.38%. The State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF is even lower at 0.35%. So that's good.

Takeaway

Presently, there's a lot going on in the defense sector. The way I view it, the chances now are elevated when it comes to a larger military budget and some sort of military conflict occurring. I don't think the probability of the latter is drastically higher. But it exists. When paired with the push that Donald Trump is making for these companies to invest more in their operations and less towards shareholders, while the end result could be a net negative in the short run, it would likely translate to more value down the road. Buying up shares of individual defense contractors can be attractive. But investing instead in defense-oriented ETFs means lower risk.

data.title: 2026 Municipal Market Outlook: High After-Tax Yields In An Uncertain World
data.description:
By Matthew Norton & Daryl Clements

High yields, a flattening curve and strong demand appear poised to propel munis.

Despite volatility, muni markets finished strong in 2025, and we are optimistic for a repeat in 2026. We expect steady investor demand and rate cuts to continue to fuel market momentum. In our view, investors who stay flexible will be best positioned to navigate volatility and seize new opportunities. And given munis' high after-tax yields, investors could be well compensated along the way.

Rate Cuts, Supply and Demand to Set the Tone

As in 2025, the path to higher returns in 2026 won’t be a straight line. We expect several factors to shape municipal market performance, starting with interest rates. A divided Federal Reserve resumed easing in December, although long-delayed jobs and inflation data led them to hint at a pause in January. Barring a major upside surprise, however, we think policymakers will eventually continue to cut—and have the room to do it.

Inflation remains high in some areas of the economy but well below 2022 peaks. Unemployment levels appear stable yet weaker, with hiring clearly slowing in some sectors. And we anticipate cooling economic growth. As a result, we expect the Fed to cut the benchmark rate to 3%, and possibly lower, over the coming year.

One potential headwind in 2026 is an increase in new issuance. Muni market new issuance hit a record $565 billion ($517 billion tax-exempt and $48 billion taxable) in 2025. According to J.P. Morgan, with municipalities facing rising capital improvement costs, issuance is likely to reach $600 billion ($545 billion tax-exempt and $55 billion taxable) in 2026.

Heavy issuance typically raises concerns about whether investor demand will keep pace. In early 2025, demand slackened as investors worried about trade-war fallout and munis’ tax-exempt status under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. But today those anxieties appear to be in the rearview mirror. Demand for municipals has surged, with inflows into muni funds reaching $50 billion in 2025.

A potentially significant tailwind: nearly $8 trillion sidelined in money market funds, waiting for re-entry. We expect a portion of that to return to bond markets over the coming two to three years as the Fed eases and cash rates fall.

When investors look for the most attractive risk-adjusted opportunities to deploy that cash, municipals are likely to top the list. The tax-equivalent yields for investment-grade and high-yield municipals are roughly 6% and 9%, respectively. For investors who pay taxes on capital gains, these yields compare favorably to returns in most other asset classes. The hunt for higher yields may push municipal bond prices higher.

Work the Curve, Target Credit

Compelling yields, a steep yield curve, strong credit fundamentals and an easing Fed continue to offer attractive entry points for muni investors. Against this backdrop, consider the following strategies:

1. Lengthen duration. Investors should consider lengthening their portfolio duration, or sensitivity to interest-rate changes, relative to the benchmark. We expect yields to fall as the economy continues to slow and the Fed revisits its rate-cutting cycle. Falling yields benefit bond prices, especially at longer durations.

2. Lift a barbell. We think it’s also a good idea to pair longer maturities with short-term bonds in a “barbell” maturity structure. In our analysis, both the short and long ends of today’s municipal yield curve are attractively valued when comparing current spreads to their five-year averages (Display).

Short- and Longer-Term Muni Valuations are Compelling, in Our View

We think investors should focus on maturities outside of 15 years, where the yield curve is especially steep (Display).

The Yield Curve is Especially Steep on the Long End

These bonds don’t only offer comparatively high yields—they also benefit from roll. When a curve is upward sloping, the yield on a bond declines as it approaches maturity. Because yield and price move in opposite directions, its price rises. The steeper the curve, the bigger the price effect from roll. Plus, if the curve flattens, which we expect, yields on those longer bonds would fall, lifting their prices.

That said, a barbell structure isn’t the right choice for every yield-curve shape, so it’s important to stay flexible.

3. Give yourself some credit. Credit—namely A-rated, BBB-rated and high-yield muni bonds—continues to offer compelling incremental income and attractive spreads, in our view.

Fundamentals remain strong; most municipalities are in good financial health, with near record-high reserves and layers of budget flexibility at their discretion.

Even so, investors should remain selective. We currently see compelling opportunities among pre-pay energy bonds, which are a type of muni revenue bond used by public utilities to lock in long-term rates with an energy provider at a discounted price. Pre-pay structures carry spreads that are meaningfully wider than comparable generic high-grade municipals—a built-in yield pickup today and the potential for future spread compression.

For similar reasons, we think Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) bonds also offer compelling opportunities. LIHTC is a federal program created in 1986 to incentivize the development and rehabilitation of affordable rental housing. LIHTC deals typically come with yields equivalent to 200–250 basis points of additional spread relative to high-grade municipals. Yet historical default rates align with investment-grade munis at around 0.5%.

We think the municipal bond market is poised for solid performance in 2026, thanks to favorable economic conditions, robust demand and anticipated rate cuts. To best take advantage of opportunities, however, investors should remain active and flexible, focusing for now on longer durations, barbell strategies and high-quality credits, and preparing for new opportunities to emerge. In today’s dynamic market, flexibility isn’t just an advantage—it’s essential.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Booz Allen Hamilton: Slight Outperformance, I'm Adding More
data.description:
If you recall, I've been a proponent of investing in Booz Allen Hamilton for a short time now - my first buy rating was earlier this year, but I doubled down in October, about 2-3 months back at the time of submission here. You can find that latest article here and see that overall, it has done about 4-5% better than the SPY in the same timeframe. Given the volatility in the market, I view this as more of an aberration than a clear outperformance.

However, the consideration that the company is appealing here is one that, in my view, is still clearly accurate. Booz Allen Hamilton has seen years of what I would consider overvaluation, and it's only in the latest 5-6 months that it's come down to a level where I would consider it buyable, if not necessarily cheap just yet.

The reason for the sharp southward move in part of the stock is clear. The cost focus and new political realities in its main market, the US market, are impacting, if not earnings on a clear level at this time, the expectations of the company's earnings. The growth impact for 2025E is expected to be a double-digit reversal, which is the first time in over a decade for this otherwise double-digit AEPS growth (annually) company. It's not a strange thing that the company, as a result of this, would crash.

However, the sheer degree of decline and crash that BAH is seeing here is one for the history books, if you'll pardon the expression. In less than 15 months, the company has cratered from a premium of 30x P/E to where I clearly considered it to be overvalued with very little potential upside at any conservative forecast, to close to a 15x P/E. That's a 50% drop in less than 15 months.

Many investors considered the company to be attractive even at 30x P/E, and if you bought at those multiples, you are in the red now - and you will likely (in my view) be in the red for quite some time.

What to Expect From Booz Allen Hamilton in 2026 - I'm Adding More

Technically, the current FX and market environment means that, to me, US companies are more attractive than in many years, at least theoretically, provided that they have a low valuation. What I mean by this is that in terms of the cost of investing at the current dollar exchange rate, my base currency (and most Western base currencies) is currently favorable to the weak (short- to medium-term) USD.

This is unfortunately coupled with a very high market valuation, which means that any attractive investment potentials at this particular time are few and far between.

Thankfully, Booz Allen Hamilton is one of those investment potentials where I can confidently say that I believe the company to be undervalued. To aid me in my analysis of the company's current prospects and future growth, we have the latest company results.

The latest results came in October, with new results still some ways off. Their trends and results can be simplified by saying that BAH is doubling down on its growth vectors while trying to manage the inherent risks to the current market and trying to "reignite growth" here.

The difficulty in doing this, unlike with a company focused on the private sector, is that BAH has trouble "creating" demand or growth here. When a government or a public institution has set a "freeze," there's usually very little that a company like this can do.

The main question for me in these situations instead becomes related to what exactly the fundamental company risk is.

My position isn't in the green yet - but close to it. My doubling down averaged my cost basis, and the growth we've seen since means I'm at about a -2% loss for my overall position. I expect this to recover with force, and I'm not worried about the company's fundamentals, despite quarterly results like the ones you see below.

I'm also no longer against the company using its cash for repurchasing shares. At a low of 10-15 years, this is actually a fairly good use of capital, and over the last 12 months, the company has bought back 2.7% of its SO with an upgraded share buyback authorization. These moves are necessary because Booz Allen Hamilton is not an attractive dividend company (with a bit of a footnote to that statement). The company typically ranges from 0.9% to 1.6%; however, as of the latest results and valuation, the yield is now actually close to 2.5%, which is at the very least a decent level.

The current guidance for the 2026E year takes into consideration negative revenue growth, updated from the previous guidance, and an EBITDA decline of about 8-9%, with a margin decline of almost 100 bps. FCF is below $1B as of the current forecast. This is a non-trivial decline, but the context is important. In this case, the context is that these are declines from very high levels of results, and as such can be viewed as more of a normalization.

I will state the company's prospects as follows. I believe independence from government contracts is an impossibility for the company - it would require the company to change its business model. Nearly the entirety of its current revenue is generated from US government contracts (Federal ones, not state-level), above all with the DoD and other intelligence agencies. This represents the potentially highest headwind, and it's what I've argued is the reason that the overvaluation could not be accepted as an attractive entry previously. That has now been "proven" in my view. The fact that budget or policy changes can affect this company as much as this means that we always need to expect it to happen and account for it in valuation.

Thankfully, now that we've entered 2026, this company is one of the arguably most attractive valuation prospects that I know in the consulting services sector, with one of the few fundamental risks being a long-term downturn for the types of contracts that the company does.

How do we account for this?

Valuation for Booz Allen Hamilton - Plenty to Like Here With a 15-25% Upside

How high do we put the company here?

I would argue that the company is worth at least 20-25x P/E. That's below its recent highs of 30x, but it takes into consideration the company's strong position and strong trends, including never missing an earnings estimate for the past 10 years, with a 10% margin of error. That means, in my view, that we can view the company's forecasts with a very high rate of likelihood going forward. Even at a low-level 20x P/E, the upside possible now is over 25% per year.

Even at a 20-year average of 18.5x, the upside here is over 18% per year, making it market-beating. It would take a significant and long-term downturn for the company to see negative returns or below a market-beating rate of return. This is the premise and expectation under which I invest in the business. Its strength lies in its predictability (which has now taken a bit of a blow but is still high), as well as its profitability. At close to $90/share, I believe the company's valuation dictates this to be a "BUY." All risks aside from the contract flow from its major contractor, the US government, such as cybersecurity threats, SCM, and AI, are, in my view, well in hand.

Analysts have varying views on the business. Quant views it as a "STRONG SELL," due to negative momentum, recent revision, and poor (comparative) growth estimates. Wall Street averages are at "HOLD." I, personally, do not see the supportive logic for such negative estimates or ratings when the likelihood of at least stable 5-9% growth or a reversal in EBITDA seems high, which at this point, it actually does. Take a look at some other estimates for the company, which already include the current contract backlog and volume.

In short, I do not agree with the quant rating or other more negative ratings but take a more contrary and proactive "BUY" rating, maintaining my PT of about $105/share, with a fair value of at the very least $130/share, based on a low level 18-20x forward P/E, owed to 8-10% average AEPS growth until 2028E.

Competitors to the company include businesses like CACI (CACI), Leidos (LDOS), and Parsons (PSN), all consulting companies in government contracting. Out of these peers, I would argue that the only one on a level as attractive as BAH is LDOS. Parsons is too volatile, and with very little growth in both 2025 and 2026, CACI hasn't declined in valuation (different subsector), but LDOS is somewhere "in between." I'd still characterize BAH as the more attractive option, however, for the sheer distance that the company has fallen here.

Risks & Considerations

We're in a risk situation where the near-term and medium-term trends for BAH are uncertain. However, this is a company that's been around for a very long time. Even in the scenario that this company has to survive on just the minimum amount compared to the usual revenue that it gets, it would survive on this.

The government can't make itself independent from consulting services (at least not realistically). It requires temporary specialized expertise, perspective (an objective one), flexibility, solutions that someone internally might not be able to offer, external risk management, and other things that external parties are more suited to offer. I know these trends intimately because I myself have worked as a government consultant for over 15 years, often with the contract goal of "minimizing" government consultant resources in the organization where I was active, ironically enough.

And while I know that these organizations can become independent to some degree, the fact remains that some tasks are much more suited to being done by government consultants rather than employees. The reasons vary - you may have a short timeframe and limited scope, extremely specialized skills, requirements for a clearly unbiased viewpoint, speed being crucial, or managing peak workloads without wanting to add new employees to long-term payroll. The fact is, these services are needed.

The risk is that the company's volumes, in the interest of minimizing governmental expenditures, are pushed to the lowest levels needed - in which case we'd probably see further declines.

Beyond that, though, I see positive upside here.

Thesis

This company is a "BUY" due to the only reason that I believe matters for the long term. Booz Allen is no longer overvalued, not even by conservative standards, as it's now under a 14-16x P/E.

Booz Allen is an extremely qualitative business with a very solid upside at the right valuation - and that is the reason I maintain my update here but change my thesis on the company overall. The recent decline, entirely justified, is nothing that changes this for me.

I believe that you may, and are likely to, outperform the market over time if you buy here, though your timeframe will have to be longer than I might have originally expected. My price target is $105/share, updated for early 2026 and the coming fiscal 3Q26. The company has a fair value of about $130/share for the long term, possibly higher if things recover more.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a valuation target of $105/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of January 2026. This company fulfills most of my investment criteria here.

data.title: Global ETF December Report
data.description:
December 2025 closed out a year marked by shifting sentiment and selective conviction across global ETF markets. Global ETF inflows reached an estimated $303 billion during the month, lifting full-year net inflows to approximately $1.5 trillion, with large-cap and broad-market equity ETFs alone attracting around $164 billion, underscoring the strength of year-end equity demand.

As inflation pressures continued to ease and central banks signalled a pause in tightening cycles, investors ended the year by rotating back into equity risk while reducing allocations to defensive assets and long-duration exposures. Equity demand was broad-based, led by core U.S. large cap equity exposures, with S&P 500 and Nasdaq ETFs attracting significant inflows as confidence in earnings resilience and expectations of prospective rate cuts strengthened.

In Europe, positive flows were driven by banking and broad-market ETFs, reflecting improving balance sheet dynamics and a more favourable funding backdrop. Across Asia-Pacific, China dominated regional activity, with record allocations into CSI A500 Index ETFs signalling renewed confidence in domestic policy support. Meanwhile, continued redemptions from money-market and long-duration bond ETFs underscored a global rotation away from cash and duration risk as the year drew to a close.

US: Mega-Caps Dominate as Investors Position for Policy Easing

December 2025 capped the year with a decisive risk-on tilt in U.S. ETF flows, as investors leaned into broad equity beta amid a supportive year-end market backdrop. U.S.-listed ETFs recorded net inflows of $241 billion during the month, marking a new record for monthly flow. Equity markets were underpinned by easing financial conditions, resilient corporate earnings and growing confidence that the Federal Reserve’s policy cycle was firmly shifting toward rate cuts in early 2026. Against this backdrop, investors prioritised scale, liquidity and simplicity, driving strong demand for core large-cap equity ETFs.

S&P 500 ETFs dominated activity, with IVV, VOO, and SPY collectively attracting over $50 billion, underscoring sustained appetite for low-cost benchmark exposure. Inflows into QQQ reflected continued conviction in U.S. growth and AI-linked mega-cap earnings, while $5 billion of inflows into VTI highlighted broad-based participation across the equity market rather than narrow sector positioning.

By contrast, December saw outflows from fixed income and select cyclical equity segments. Corporate bond ETFs and ultra-short Treasury exposures faced redemptions as investors reduced defensive allocations and rotated away from cash-like instruments.

Europe: Targeted Equity Positioning Amid Rising Rate-Cut Confidence

European ETF flows in December pointed to a selective risk-on rotation, as investors balanced improving macro sentiment with continued caution around the policy outlook. European-listed equity ETFs led net inflows of $30 billion, supported by easing inflation pressures and greater confidence in earnings visibility heading into 2026. Broad European equity exposure remained in demand, while financial sector ETFs, particularly banks, stood out as beneficiaries of expectations that profitability would remain resilient even as rate cuts draw closer.

Money market ETFs also continued to attract inflows, underscoring a dual allocation approach that combined increased equity exposure with active short-term liquidity management. This reflected investors’ desire to remain flexible while gradually increasing risk exposure. In contrast, fixed income ETFs experienced notable outflows, led by corporate credit and short-dated government bond products. Asian ETF markets closed 2025 with a clear shift toward equity exposure, underpinned by China’s late-year rally and steady momentum in Japan. The China Southern and Huatai PineBridge products led gains, reflecting renewed investor confidence in China’s policy support and prospects for growth recovery.

At the same time, investors reduced cash and duration exposure, signalling a move away from capital preservation strategies. Money market ETFs saw notable redemptions, with the Kodex Money Market ETF recording net outflows of approximately $1.3 billion, while bond markets softened modestly amid rising inflation expectations.

Overall, December flows pointed to a constructive shift in risk appetite across Asia-Pacific, as investors favoured large-cap equity exposure and trimmed defensive allocations heading into 2026. Summary of Monthly Flows

December closed the year with investors using ETFs to adjust portfolios as confidence grew around an upcoming shift in monetary policy. As inflation eased and expectations for rate cuts strengthened, investors reduced defensive positions and increased exposure to broad equity markets, while trimming allocations to cash-like products and long-duration bonds.

Flow patterns varied by region but shared a more constructive tone. In the US, strong inflows reflected confidence in corporate earnings, with investors favouring large-cap benchmark ETFs. In Europe, investors selectively added equity exposure, particularly in financials and broad-market ETFs, supported by improving balance sheets and funding conditions. In Asia-Pacific, flows were more concentrated, led by China equity ETFs as expectations for policy support increased, alongside steady participation in other regional markets.

Overall, December flows reinforced the role of ETFs as efficient and flexible allocation tools, allowing investors to adjust regional and asset-class exposures while positioning for a more supportive policy environment in 2026.

data.title: What Earnings Explain, And What They Don't: Insights From 150 Years Of Market Data
data.description:
By Terri Campbell, CFA

Stock prices and corporate earnings move closely together over long horizons, a relationship confirmed by more than a century of data compiled by Robert Shiller. This analysis examines the strength of that long-term linkage and tests whether changes in the earnings–price correlation offer insight into future stock market returns.

The results show that while earnings help explain market behavior over time, fluctuations in the correlation itself do not provide a useful basis for forecasting returns. The sections that follow document empirical patterns across multiple rolling periods and assess the limits of using correlation measures as market-timing tools. The findings may also help financial advisors frame long-term market behavior for clients in a grounded and intuitive way.

What This Analysis Aims to Clarify

I examine the long-term relationship between stock prices and corporate earnings for two main reasons.

First, the findings offer a straightforward way to explain stock market behavior over long investment horizons. I define a long horizon as more than 10 years, which is a useful minimum timeframe for retirement planning and for making asset allocation decisions.

Second, after calculating the correlations between prices and earnings, I tested whether changes in the correlation over time might serve as a leading indicator of future returns. Specifically, I asked whether periods of unusually low historical correlation were followed by stronger or weaker subsequent stock market performance.

Correlation Results

The analysis uses monthly averages of the S&P Composite earnings per share and the S&P Composite price. The reported monthly earnings, stock price, and returns data for the S&P Composite companies are based on Shiller’s data from 1871 through December 2024.

Across multiple time periods, the correlations between earnings and prices were consistently high.

Time Period Correlation
Full data set (01/1871 – 12/2024) 0.977
100 years (01/1925 – 12/2024) 0.974
Post-1940 Investors Act (08/1940 – 04/2024) 0.973
50 Years (01/1975 – 12/2024) 0.963

I chose common time periods to examine the data and note the following:

  • One starting point is the 1940 Investors Act, used to test whether results differed after investor protections and more uniform accounting standards were introduced. The difference appears negligible.
  • The past 10- and 20-year periods were included to reflect what is often considered a typical retirement-planning horizon.

Correlation Changes Over Time

The correlation between earnings and stock prices does fluctuate over time, particularly across shorter horizons such as the five-, 10-, and 20-year windows. The rolling 50-year correlations also vary, though within a much narrower range.

The lowest rolling 50-year correlation occurred during the first half of the 20th century, when the data series reached 0.6. Given the backdrop of two world wars, the Great Depression, and limited market regulation prior to 1940, it is notable that the correlation did not fall further.

Variability increased as the time horizon shortened. In the rolling 20-year series, correlations fell below 0.50 for a full decade between February 1918 and December 1928, and again briefly in December 1948.

The rolling 10-year correlations fell below zero during three periods: at the end of World War I and World War II, and during the high inflation era of the late 1970s and early 1980s.

Rolling five-year correlations naturally showed the most volatility, with deeper drops and more frequent swings, including multiple periods of negative correlation. Both the average and median rolling five-year correlations were lower than those observed over longer horizons.

Does the Variability in Correlations Correspond with Returns?

To test whether variation in the earnings–price correlation has any predictive value for stock returns, we ran regressions of correlation levels against subsequent annualized returns.

The R² between S&P Composite earnings and price from 1871 through 2024 is very high at 0.95. Given the strength of this long-term relationship—and the relative rarity of low-correlation periods—it is reasonable to ask whether those periods might function as buy or sell signals. In other words, does variation in the earnings–price correlation help predict future returns?

I evaluated this question across multiple rolling time horizons. The resulting R² values — linking correlation levels to subsequent annualized returns — were far lower than the R² between earnings and price themselves. For the rolling 10-year and five-year windows, the R² fell close to zero, indicating virtually no predictive relationship.

The rolling 50-year period showed the strongest relationship with a R2 of 0.53.

For the rolling 20-year windows, the R² was 0.24, reflecting considerably more variability.

Variability increased further in the rolling 10-year series, where the R² fell to 0.06.

The rolling five-year periods show no consistent pattern. R2 is nearly 0.0 (actual: 1.27E-07).

Overall, I found no evidence that changes in the earnings–price correlation predict future annualized returns. The data show that the two measures do not move together in any meaningful way for horizons shorter than 50 years.

Predictive Power of Correlation

The strong long-term relationship between earnings and prices offers a clear explanation for the rise and fall of stock markets over extended periods. It provides a simple and intuitive framework for understanding long-run equity trends.

However, the second goal – determining whether changes in the correlation could serve as a predictive measure for annualized returns – was not achieved. The evidence suggests that other factors beyond the earnings–price relationship drive the rate of change in annualized returns, even though the two series move closely together over long horizons.

Key Takeaways

  • Earnings and stock prices move closely together over long horizons. More than 150 years of Shiller data show a consistently strong relationship between the two series.
  • Shorter windows introduce substantial noise. Correlations fluctuate meaningfully over five-, 10-, and 20-year periods, reflecting wars, inflation shocks, and structural changes.
  • Correlation strength does not imply predictive power. Shifts in the earnings–price correlation have little ability to forecast subsequent returns at horizons relevant to most investors.
  • Only the longest windows show limited explanatory power. Even the 50-year regressions, with an R² of 0.53, offer only modest insight, while shorter horizons fall close to zero.

Earnings help explain long-term market behavior, but they do not help time the market.

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

data.title: Lanxess Is Slogging Through A Painful Downturn, But The Cycle Will Recover Eventually
data.description:
Whatever progress LANXESS (LNXSF) (LNXSY) (LXSG.DE) had been making in convincing the Street that it had turned a corner with its operations and capability to maintain through-the-cycle profits is basically gone now. The macro environment went from bad to worse throughout 2025, and an influx of cheap imports drove utilization to levels that cannot sustain attractive profits and cash flow generation for this large German chemicals company.

LANXESS' shares are down about 40% since my last update (the ADRs have done a little better due to currency), meaningfully underperforming a weak chemicals sector that has broadly seen the same macro challenges, demand evaporation, and export competition, with only some respite from lower input costs.

It’s tempting to argue that conditions can’t get much worse for LANXESS, and I do think it’s noteworthy that in a terrible year that has seen utilization fall below 70% (typically 85% is where margins really inflect positively), EBITDA margin is still likely to come in at the high single digits and the company could still generate some positive free cash flow. On the other hand, next year is likely to be almost as challenging, and any value here may require a line of sight to volume growth to surface.

A Painful Year… And No Obvious Respite In Sight

As I said in the open, 2025 was a terrible year. I outlined some concerns I had about the macro environment early in the year, but pretty much all that could have gone wrong did go wrong.

Agriculture (around 20% of the business) was poor throughout most of the year, with weaker commodity prices making affordability an issue and export competition from Asia vying for what demand there was. Energy industry demand was likewise soft throughout much of the year, while autos and construction weakened as the year went on.

All told, somewhere in the neighborhood of 66% to 75% of LANXESS’s revenue base saw meaningful weakness throughout 2025, and while food and consumer goods performed somewhat better, there wasn’t much real growth there, and as I discussed in a recent article on Innospec (IOSP), there was widespread margin pressure in consumer-related products due to input cost pressure and minimal pricing power.

With buyers worried about the macro environment and reportedly only buying for immediate needs (as opposed to buying multiple months’ worth at a time), volumes plunged. While LANXESS started the year off okay with 2% volume growth, that reversed to 4% contraction in Q2 and over 6% contraction in Q3, and given a +3% comp for the upcoming fourth quarter, I’m not expecting good news here.

These weak volumes have driven utilization down to 67%, and it’s tough for any chemical company to generate attractive margins at that level.

Unfortunately it doesn’t look like a V-shaped recovery is in sight. German chemicals group VCI is looking for industry volumes to decline another 3.5% in 2026, and that is likely to put even more pressure on utilization and margins. I do expect some recovery in construction in the second half (helped in part by German fiscal stimulus and lower rates in the U.S.), but agriculture and autos are likely to remain pressured, and I don’t see other end-markets like chemicals or energy picking up that slack.

Monetizing Envalior Will Help… But It May Take A While

LANXESS announced in late September that it had started the process to monetize its 40% stake in the Envalior JV. Under the terms of the JV, Advent is obligated to acquire LANXESS’s stake at a 12x EBITDA multiple, implying a value of around EUR 1.2B against a roughly EUR 3.9B EV for LANXESS today.

There are some important “buts” to the monetization process. First, it’s not a given that LANXESS will be able to sell the entire stake and may only be able to sell half. Second, even that may not happen—the sale process is subject to Advent securing financing on acceptable terms, and given the challenges in the chemicals industry today, that may not be possible. LANXESS does have the right to sell the stake on April 1, 2028 irrespective of financing, but it may well prove to be the case that LANXESS has to wait another two years to monetize this asset—certainly the market’s valuation would suggest that to be the case.

Self-Help Can Only Do So Much

A terrible 2025 has cast a shadow over the progress that LANXESS has been making. The company has successfully diversified its business, reducing its former overreliance on the auto and tire industries, and has exited some less attractive segments like leather chemicals and synthetic rubber. Management has also been taking costs out of the business and restructuring, selling, or exiting businesses that it can’t bring up to adequate levels of performance.

LANXESS has also increased its global diversification, adding more exposure to North America and benefitting from lower feedstock costs there, and has generally reduced its cyclicality and increased exposure to markets with longer-term attractive characteristics (including agriculture, consumer goods, and electronics).

As 2025 shows, though, those moves can only help so much when demand disappears and foreign exporters dump bulk chemicals they can’t use into the global market.

Management is moving forward with another cost reduction program (worth around EUR 100M) in addition to previously-announced process and production efficiency improvement efforts. Cutting costs by EUR 100M isn’t trivial next to around EUR 500M in expected EBITDA, and management is moving to close inefficient plants in the face of excess capacity.

Other positive drivers could offer some incremental upside over the next 12-24 months, including a more stable tariff situation, German fiscal stimulus working its way into the economy, and anti-dumping actions against imports. None of those will replace underlying demand improvement, though, and that is not likely to develop until the second half of 2026 at best.

The Outlook

Weak pricing and volumes have been a one-two punch to revenue, and where I was once looking for low single-digit revenue growth, it’s now looking like LANXESS will see a roughly 10% year-over-year decline in revenue. Weak utilization (from typical levels in the high 70%s to the high 60%s now) has magnified the pain on the EBITDA line, with full-year margin likely to slip below 9% instead of the 11% I’d previously expected.

Looking ahead, I don’t expect a dramatic demand improvement in 2026, as I think that second-half recovery will have to start from a lower base given ongoing weakness in the first half of the year. Said differently, LANXESS will be paddling hard in 2026 just to stay in basically the same place. I do expect a more meaningful recovery in 2027, though, and I do think 3% to 4% long-term revenue growth beyond that is achievable (and likely well above that in particularly strong recovery years when there is limited spare capacity and more pricing power).

When it comes to cyclical companies, it’s tempting to see both the good times and the bad times as the new normal, and many sell-side analysts are modeling LANXESS as if margins will never meaningfully recover where they were once expecting improvement into the low-to-mid-teens (for EBITDA) on the basis of those cost efficiency and business diversification moves. I do think this has been a painful reset for the story, but I do think EBITDA margin can get back above 10% when the business recovers and ultimately see expansion toward the low-to-mid teens.

That, in turn, should push free cash flow margins back toward the 3%-4% range over the next five years and ultimately drive around 6% long-term FCF growth.

Discounting those cash flows back suggests around 30% upside today, and I get a similar result with a 7.5x multiple on my FY’26 EBITDA estimate. While that is above the long-term average forward EBITDA multiple, that’s appropriate in a cyclical trough, and I get a similar result with a 6.5x multiple (in line with the long-term average) on my estimate of normalized full-cycle EBITDA.

The Bottom Line

Roughly 30% upside would normally argue for a positive stance on the shares, and that’s before factoring in the potential impact of the Envalior stake monetization (if it happens in 2026). Still, I’m hesitant to argue for buying these shares when volumes are still so weak, the outlook is not particularly encouraging, and management’s estimates keep coming down. This is a name I intend to watch more closely, though, as I do see worthwhile upside whenever this cyclical downturn peters out and a recovery cycle begins.

data.title: The Power Of Hedging In A Year Of Yen Swings
data.description:
By Hyun Kang

The yen experienced a dramatic round-trip in 2025.

In the spring, at the height of the "sell America" trade, the dollar was down more than 10% versus the yen.

As that trade faded, and as a new Prime Minister in Japan raised expectations of a weaker yen, the dollar recouped nearly all those losses.

At the time of this writing, the yen remains modestly stronger, just less than 1% year-to-date.

Figure 1: USD/JPY Spot Rate

Investing in foreign companies always comes with foreign currency risk.

Hedging the currency exposure—for example, selling the yen short in a forward currency contract—can neutralize the yen exposure within an equity allocation. In a year when the yen appreciates, currency hedging would typically be a headwind relative to unhedged exposures, as you don't get the benefit of the yen strengthening.

But 2025 is a unique example and really highlights a case for investors who don't have a strong view on the yen.

In a year when the yen is basically flat to slightly up, our currency-hedged Japan Dividend Index has outperformed its unhedged counterpart by more than 400 basis points (bps).

How, you ask? Because currency hedging not only aims to replicate local equity returns but also indirectly enhances returns via interest rate carry. When short-term rates in the U.S. are higher than those of the foreign currency, a U.S. investor earns the interest rate differential by being long the dollar and short the foreign currency.

As shown in the chart below, the WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Index, tracked by the WisdomTree Japan Hedged Equity Fund (DXJ), returned more than 400 bps this year compared to the local currency equity index and more than 600 bps compared to its benchmark, the MSCI Japan Local Index, while the MSCI Japan Index returned 54 bps less in local returns than USD.

Figure 2: Year-to-Date Returns

DXJ's performance leadership isn't limited to 2025. As the chart below shows, DXJ's Index has outpaced the S&P 500 across the one-, three-, five- and seven-year periods, an underappreciated trend that challenges the idea that Japan is only a tactical trade. This multiyear advantage reinforces the case for viewing Japan, and DXJ specifically, as a strategic allocation rather than a short-term opportunity.

Figure 3: Annualized Total Returns

The case for DXJ goes beyond performance. Driven by strong corporate governance reforms and efforts to attract retail investors by the Tokyo Stock Exchange, Japanese companies have substantially hiked dividends and are on track to pay record total dividends for 2025.

Figure 4: Dividend Growth

During most years following its inception, DXJ's Index lagged the S&P 500 Index in terms of shareholder yield, which we define as the sum of dividend yield and share reduction yield. There has since been a reversal of that trend in recent years, and the Index currently yields more than two percentage points more than the S&P 500.

Figure 5: Historical Shareholder Yield

Importantly, this shift in shareholder yield has occurred while valuations remain meaningfully below those of U.S. equities. DXJ's Index currently trades at a significant discount to the S&P 500 Index—13 price-to-earnings (P/E) turns lower—while also delivering superior returns and paying shareholders more by every metric.

Figure 6: Fundamentals

Conclusion: A Japanese Renaissance

Japan's market story in 2025 shows how much currency swings can shape investor outcomes and how effective hedging can be in navigating them. DXJ's strong performance this year, alongside its consistent leadership over longer horizons, reflects a renewed appreciation for Japan as more than a tactical allocation. For investors who do not have a strong view on the yen, DXJ offers a straightforward, strategic way to maintain exposure to Japan's equity market while reducing the uncertainty that comes from FX volatility.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. The Fund focuses its investments in Japan, thereby increasing the impact of events and developments in Japan that can adversely affect performance. Investments in currency involve additional special risks, such as credit risk and interest rate fluctuations. Derivative investments can be volatile and may be less liquid than other securities and more sensitive to the effects of varied economic conditions. As this Fund can have a high concentration in some issuers, the Fund can be adversely impacted by changes affecting those issuers. Due to the investment strategy of this Fund, it may make higher capital gain distributions than other ETFs. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Hyun Kang, Research Analyst

Hyun Kang joined WisdomTree in July 2022 as a Research Analyst. As a part of the Index team, he assists with the creation and maintenance of the firm’s indexes and supports the group’s research initiatives across various strategies. Hyun graduated from Carnegie Mellon University, with a B.S. in Business Administration and an additional major in Statistics and Machine Learning.
